title: Leaking YouTube creators' private videos
titleZh: 泄露YouTube创作者的私密视频
author: javxfps
relevanceReason: 该帖子讨论了YouTube创作者私密视频泄露的安全漏洞问题，涉及平台安全和隐私保护，属于安全领域。
summaryRaw: Leaking YouTube Creators Private Videos | Javox
summaryZh: YouTube存在私密视频泄露漏洞
创作者的私密内容可能被未授权访问
highlightsZh: 安全漏洞导致隐私泄露

comments.author: Mg6yDfjp5U
comments.textRawHtml:
I recently left Google having worked on a number of projects with various YouTube teams. I think I can explain why it's being handled this way by YouTube.

This is a fairly nuanced/involved issue, so the task of classifying the bug likely made it's way to one of the engineers responsible for the implementation of this feature.

That engineer has already launched this project, and filed it away under their GRAD (performance) artifacts for when promo/annual review talks roll around. There's no motivation for this engineer to waste time fixing this bug because it won't benefit their promo packet, and they are already being put under pressure to launch other projects which _will_ benefit their promo packet.

So they do what they can to sweep it under the rug because that's what the promo/annual review framework (GRAD) incentivizes and rewards.
comments.textZhHtml:
我最近离开 Google，与多个 YouTube 团队一起参与了多个项目。我想我可以解释为什么 YouTube 会这样处理。

这是一个相当微妙/涉及的问题，因此对错误进行分类的任务可能会交给负责实现此功能的工程师之一。

该工程师已经启动了这个项目，并将其归档在他们的 GRAD（性能）工件下，以便在促销/年度审查会谈进行时使用。这位工程师没有动力浪费时间修复这个错误，因为这对他们的促销包没有好处，而且他们已经面临启动其他项目的压力，这些项目_将_有利于他们的促销包。

因此，他们尽其所能将其掩盖起来，因为这就是促销/年度审查框架（GRAD）的激励和奖励。

children.author: throwrioawfo
children.textRawHtml:
I feel like things have become so much more cynical in the last 5 years, in this regard.

I feel like part of it is the "over-systemization" of promos. I see the logic behind it to some extent - if there's a system, it's "fairer"/"more democratic". But, then we end up with ridiculous gamified promo systems.
children.textZhHtml:
我觉得在这方面，过去5年里事情变得愈发愤世嫉俗了。

我觉得部分原因在于晋升的“过度系统化”。我在某种程度上能理解这背后的逻辑——如果有套系统，就会显得“更公平”/“更民主”。但结果却是，我们搞出了荒谬的游戏化晋升系统。

children.author: campbel
children.textRawHtml:
objective systems become gamified

subjective systems become politicized

pick your poison
children.textZhHtml:
客观的系统会被游戏化

主观的系统会被政治化

只能两害相权取其轻了

children.author: ismailmaj
children.textRawHtml: I'll pick small company, thank you.

children.author: bartread
children.textRawHtml: This isn’t a bad approach but it’s not a panacea: small companies can be pretty messed up too, albeit perhaps in different ways.
children.textZhHtml: 这方法不算差，但也并非万能药：小公司同样可能一团糟，只是表现形式或许不同罢了。

children.author: manquer
children.textRawHtml:
The impact is local though, it would be only a problem if the median small company is more messed up than the large co.

It not likely to happen because being small there are more threats or market forces to deal with so they cannot do as they please. Monopolies or just economies of scale affords large co and the small number of executives that control them outsized influence - both good and bad.
children.textZhHtml:
不过这种影响是局部的，只有当一般的小公司比大公司更混乱时，这才会成为一个问题。

这种情况不太可能发生，因为规模小意味着要应对更多的威胁或市场力量，所以他们不能随心所欲。垄断或仅仅是规模经济赋予了大公司以及控制它们的少数高管过大的影响力——无论好坏。

children.author: doctorpangloss
children.textRawHtml: Yeah... there are no systems that are not political. Even if you agree objectivity is a thing, someone has to persuade others to buy into whatever that objectivity is, and that's still politics, and not cynical at all.
children.textZhHtml: 是啊……没有不带有政治色彩的系统。即使你认同客观性确实存在，也得有人去说服别人接受这种客观性，这本身依然是政治，而且一点也不愤世嫉俗。

children.author: anonymars
children.textRawHtml: This is great.  I'd begun to conclude the pendulum swung too far towards "moneyball" and both approaches have trade-offs, but this is perfectly succinct
children.textZhHtml: 这说得太好了。我本来已经开始觉得钟摆过度偏向“魔球理论”了，而且这两种方法都有各自的取舍，但这段总结简直再精辟不过了。

children.author: lacunary
children.textRawHtml: it is both because the "objective" system is also rife with subjective judgements
children.textZhHtml: 两者皆是，因为“客观”的体系同样充斥着主观判断

children.author: ikiris
children.textRawHtml: 5 years ago they had the same incentives.
children.textZhHtml: 5年前他们的激励机制也是一样的。

children.author: tmoertel
children.textRawHtml: But five years ago they had a stronger engineering culture. The old values were rapidly eroding, but some still held.
children.textZhHtml: 但五年前他们的工程文化要浓厚得多。旧有的价值观虽然在迅速流失，但有些依然被坚守着。

children.author: tgma
children.textRawHtml: It's funny whenever you talk to anyone their "five years ago" maps almost to the time they joined the company.
children.textZhHtml: 有趣的是，每当你和别人交谈时，他们口中的“五年前”几乎正好对应他们加入公司的时间。

children.author: wahnfrieden
children.textRawHtml: It’s not about fairness or democracy (maybe you meant meritocracy?) at all although it’s sold that way to participants - it’s primarily about ownership’s ability to cascade management duties, including mitigating latent negotiation powers by individual workers and groups of workers
children.textZhHtml: 这根本与公平或民主无关（也许你想说的是优绩主义？），尽管它是这样向参与者包装推销的——它主要关乎所有者逐级下放管理职责的能力，包括削弱个别员工和员工群体潜在的谈判权力

children.author: jambalaya8
children.textRawHtml: Eh, clearcut promo paths used to be a bigger thing in the 90s and they did work for a little while, they just didn't handle exceptions well, and then the whole developed world up and thought they were also exceptions. Certifications used to matter more, now they are so cheapened that you cannot do much without them.
children.textZhHtml: 唉，在90年代，明确的晋升路径曾更受重视，而且确实管用了一段时间，只是它们处理例外情况的能力不佳，然后整个发达世界就纷纷觉得自己也是例外了。证书过去更重要，现在含金量已经大打折扣，但没有它们你又几乎寸步难行。

children.author: brailsafe
children.textRawHtml:
> This is a prime example of why programmers are not seriously considered engineers.

Seems to me like your comment is simply an example of prejudice.

You're just describing another standardized incentive structure that you're operating in, and using that as a basis to extrapolate that programmers of all kinds—whether they work on a video platform or on machinery that could cause catastrophe if it fails—are implicitly careless careerists who refuse responsibility by nature.
children.textZhHtml:
> 这是为什么程序员不被认真视为工程师的一个典型例子。

在我看来，你的评论只是一个偏见的例子。

你只是描述了你正在使用的另一种标准化激励结构，并以此为基础来推断各种程序员——无论他们在视频平台上工作，还是在一旦失败可能导致灾难的机器上工作——都是隐含的粗心的野心家，拒绝承担责任本质上。

children.author: digdugdirk
children.textRawHtml:
Other fields of engineering usually have a regulated licensure, upon which they can call themselves a Professional Engineer. This gives them the ability to make final approval/sign-off on designs and technical reports. It's most common in civil engineering, where a PE license is required for all publicly funded projects (and most privately funded ones as well, due to local/regional/national regulations) to be approved.

This license requires the holder to uphold code of professional ethics, and makes the engineer themselves be personally responsible for the safety and viability of the design itself. Losing a PE license is rare, but it does happen. The industry board (usually a regional board) can also discipline/reprimand engineers who fail to meet the professional standard - rubber stamping projects, personal misconduct, etc. Losing a license is a huge deal, but even reprimands can have a serious negative impact on someone's career.

In the industry the previous commenter works in their hypothetical would absolutely meet the bar for discipline or reprimand.
children.textZhHtml:
其他工程领域通常拥有受监管的许可证，据此他们可以称自己为专业工程师。这使他们能够对设计和技术报告进行最终批准/签署。这种情况在土木工程中最为常见，所有公共资助的项目（以及大多数私人资助的项目，由于当地/地区/国家法规的规定，也需要 PE 许可证）才能获得批准。

该许可证要求持有者遵守职业道德准则，并要求工程师本人对设计本身的安全性和可行性负责。失去 PE 许可证的情况很少见，但确实会发生。行业委员会（通常是地区委员会）还可以对不符合专业标准的工程师进行纪律处分/谴责——橡皮图章项目、个人不当行为等。失去许可证是一件大事，但即使是谴责也会对某人的职业生涯产生严重的负面影响。

在行业中，之前的评论者在他们的假设中工作绝对符合纪律或谴责的标准。
